summary: Gruber responds to a Wall Street Journal story claiming Apple board members had informal discussions with executive recruiters about CEO succession. He argues the story's timing—published 30 minutes before Apple's record earnings—was deliberately designed to dampen positive stock movement. He systematically dismantles every proposed external candidate for Apple CEO, from Eric Schmidt to Jack Dorsey, arguing none has credibility with both Apple employees and Wall Street. His conclusion is straightforward: Tim Cook is the only realistic successor, and a post-Jobs Apple would essentially be Apple as it already operates, minus Jobs himself.
key_points: The WSJ succession story was sketchily sourced with zero named individuals and suspiciously timed to undercut Apple's record earnings announcement
No outsider could credibly replace Jobs because Apple employees value design above all else, and no other tech company shares that ethos
A successful Apple CEO must satisfy two disparate groups simultaneously: Apple's design-oriented rank-and-file and Wall Street's financial expectations
Tim Cook is the only realistic successor—he already runs the company operationally, has Wall Street credibility, and understands why Apple succeeds
The obvious post-Jobs structure is simply Apple as it already works, with Cook as CEO and Forstall, Ive, and Schiller holding final word on product design
money_quotes: There is no one from outside Apple who could credibly replace Jobs as CEO of Apple.
Apple employees respect and cultivate great design above all else. No other company in technology reflects this ethos. There is no other company like Apple.
There is a better chance of Apple choosing its next CEO through a raffle of ten golden tickets hidden inside iPad boxes distributed around the globe than that they'd give the job to Eric Schmidt.
There will not be a next Steve Jobs. There will be a next CEO.
He's an operations guy who understands how Apple works and why it has succeeded, and what it needs to keep doing to continue growing.
The last thing Apple needs is a shake-up or a dose of what any of their competitors are drinking.
Put another way, the obvious structure for a post-Jobs Apple is simply Apple as we know it, without Steve Jobs.
Cook has so much credibility on Wall Street that effectively, the board might have to offer him the job.
key_insight: The question of Apple CEO succession is far less interesting than the media wants it to be—Tim Cook is the only credible choice because no outsider commands respect from both Apple's design-obsessed workforce and Wall Street.
